section.title: The Proverbs of Solomon
section.sectionRationale: A new heading, 'The proverbs of Solomon,' marks the start of the book's first great collection of short, independent sayings, most of them built on antithetic parallelism, a righteous line set directly against a wicked one. Chapter 10 gathers thirty two such sayings around recurring themes, honest speech, diligent hands, and the LORD's blessing on the upright.
section.thesis: Where chapters 1 to 9 built sustained arguments, this new collection teaches wisdom proverb by proverb, contrasting the righteous and the wicked so sharply and repeatedly that the reader begins to see two whole ways of life, in work, speech, and destiny, standing behind every line.
section.frameNote: Four thematic clusters within the chapter's independent sayings: the wise and diligent household, verses 1 to 5; the righteous and wicked in speech, verses 6 to 14; wealth, work, and the tongue, verses 15 to 21; and the LORD's blessing and the destinies of the righteous and the wicked, verses 22 to 32.

verses.text: The proverbs of Solomon: A wise son brings joy to his father, but a foolish son grief to his mother.
Ill-gotten treasures profit nothing, but righteousness brings deliverance from death.
The LORD does not let the {{tsaddiq|righteous}} go hungry, but He denies the craving of the {{rasha|wicked}}.
Idle hands make one poor, but diligent hands bring wealth.
He who gathers in summer is a wise son, but he who sleeps during harvest is a disgraceful son.
Blessings are on the head of the righteous, but the mouth of the wicked conceals violence.
The memory of the righteous is a blessing, but the name of the wicked will rot.
A wise heart will receive commandments, but foolish lips will come to ruin.
He who walks in integrity walks securely, but he who perverts his ways will be found out.
He who winks the eye causes grief, and foolish lips will come to ruin.
The mouth of the righteous is a fountain of life, but the mouth of the wicked {{kesah|conceals}} violence.
Hatred stirs up dissension, but love {{kesah|covers}} all transgressions.
Wisdom is found on the lips of the discerning, but a rod is for the back of him who lacks judgment.
The wise store up knowledge, but the mouth of the fool invites destruction.

units.label: Wealth, work, and the tongue
units.note: the ordinary economy of a life

verses.text: The wealth of the rich man is his fortified city, but poverty is the ruin of the poor.
The labor of the righteous leads to life, but the gain of the wicked brings punishment.
Whoever heeds instruction is on the path to life, but he who ignores reproof goes astray.
The one who conceals hatred has lying lips, and whoever spreads slander is a fool.
When words are many, sin is unavoidable, but he who restrains his lips is wise.
The tongue of the righteous is choice silver, but the heart of the wicked has little worth.
The lips of the righteous feed many, but fools die for lack of judgment.

units.label: The LORD's blessing and two destinies

verses.text: The blessing of the LORD enriches, and He adds no sorrow to it.
The fool delights in shameful conduct, but a man of understanding has wisdom.
What the wicked man dreads will overtake him, but the desire of the righteous will be granted.
When the whirlwind passes, the wicked are no more, but the righteous are secure forever.
Like vinegar to the teeth and smoke to the eyes, so is the slacker to those who send him.
The {{yirah|fear of the LORD}} prolongs life, but the years of the wicked will be cut short.
The hope of the righteous is joy, but the expectations of the wicked will perish.
The way of the LORD is a refuge to the upright, but destruction awaits those who do evil.
The righteous will never be shaken, but the wicked will not inhabit the land.
The mouth of the righteous brings forth wisdom, but a perverse tongue will be cut out.
The lips of the righteous know what is fitting, but the mouth of the wicked is perverse.

tsaddiq.gloss: The righteous one, a person whose life is rightly ordered toward God and neighbor. Chapter 10 sets this figure against the wicked in almost every verse, showing righteousness less as flawless perfection and more as a settled, trustworthy direction of life.

rasha.gloss: The wicked one, whose life is bent against God's order, described here through hidden violence, hollow gain, and a name that will rot. The chapter's relentless contrast with the righteous is meant to make each reader ask which category truly describes them.

kesah.gloss: To cover or conceal, used twice in close proximity with opposite force, the wicked mouth conceals violence, verse 11, while love covers transgressions, verse 12. The same Hebrew root can hide evil or graciously overlook another's wrong, and the New Testament quotes verse 12 directly, 1 Peter 4:8.

yirah.gloss: The fear of the LORD, named in verse 27 as the very thing that prolongs life, tying this chapter's many contrasts of righteous and wicked back to the book's opening and recurring motto.

basic.context: With chapter 10, Proverbs shifts style entirely. Instead of long connected speeches, it now offers a rapid string of short, independent sayings, most built on a simple pattern, a line about the righteous set directly against a line about the wicked. Read together, these thirty two proverbs paint two whole trajectories of life, in family, work, speech, and ultimate destiny, and press the reader to notice which trajectory actually describes them.
basic.hermeneutics: Each verse here is a self contained proverb, not a paragraph in a developing argument, so the same theme, righteous versus wicked speech, work, or destiny, is revisited from many angles rather than argued once at length. These are general, typical observations about how life under God usually unfolds, not legal guarantees; the book itself, and Job and Ecclesiastes alongside it, shows that the righteous sometimes suffer and the wicked sometimes prosper for a season, without overturning the truth that God's moral order is real and ultimately vindicated.
basic.originalLanguages: Nearly every verse sets the tsaddiq, righteous, against the rasha, wicked, letting repetition do the teaching work a sustained argument would otherwise do. Verses 11 and 12 use the same root, kacah, to cover or conceal, for opposite ends, the wicked mouth conceals violence, while love covers transgressions, a wordplay the New Testament picks up directly, 1 Peter 4:8. And the chapter's closing section returns to yirah, the fear of the LORD, verse 27, tying its many contrasts back to the book's foundational motto.
basic.typology: Verse 12's picture of love covering all transgressions anticipates and is directly quoted in 1 Peter 4:8, and ultimately points to the greater covering of sin accomplished at the cross, where Christ's love did not merely overlook wrongdoing between neighbors but actually bore its penalty so it could be truly and finally covered.
basic.christ: The chapter's recurring picture of the righteous as secure, blessed, and a fountain of life, while the wicked face ruin, finds its deepest ground in Christ, the only truly righteous One, whose righteousness alone qualifies anyone to stand in the blessed category this chapter describes. And verse 12, love covers all transgressions, quoted directly in 1 Peter 4:8, points to a love that does not merely overlook sin between neighbors but, in Christ, actually bears its cost so that it can be truly covered forever.
basic.god: God is the source of every genuine blessing, verse 22, adding no sorrow to what He gives, and He is the moral foundation beneath this whole chapter's contrasts, the LORD does not let the righteous go hungry, verse 3, the way of the LORD is a refuge to the upright, verse 29. His fear, not mere cleverness, is what actually prolongs life, verse 27.
basic.crossReferences: 1 Peter 4:8 quotes verse 12 nearly word for word, above all, love covers a multitude of sins. Matthew 12:34 to 37 teaches that words reveal the heart, echoing this chapter's many sayings on speech. Psalm 1 pictures the same two ways, righteous and wicked, that structure this whole chapter. Romans 5:19 grounds true righteousness in Christ's obedience, not human achievement.
basic.oneStory: This chapter's rapid fire contrasts between the righteous and the wicked summarize, in miniature, the whole Bible's story of two ways, one leading to life, one to ruin. That story finds its resolution in Christ, whose perfect righteousness alone qualifies sinners for the blessings this chapter describes, and whose covering love, quoted directly in the New Testament from verse 12, deals fully and finally with the sin no human effort can cover on its own.

context.literary: The heading 'The proverbs of Solomon,' verse 1, marks the start of the book's first great collection of independent sayings, running through chapter 22:16, built overwhelmingly on antithetic parallelism, a first line and a contrasting second line, a style entirely different from the sustained discourses of chapters 1 to 9.
context.historical: The everyday concerns voiced here, a father's joy or grief over a son, verse 1, food security, verse 3, harvest labor, verse 5, honest speech in a small community, verses 18 to 19, reflect the practical, agrarian, household centered world of ancient Israel, where reputation and daily provision depended heavily on personal character and diligence.
context.canonical: This antithetic proverb form, righteous against wicked, echoes the two way structure of Psalm 1 and anticipates Jesus' own teaching on two paths, two builders, and two trees bearing good or bad fruit, Matthew 7:13-27, showing this pattern of biblical ethics running from the Psalms through the Gospels.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To teach wisdom through rapid, memorable contrasts between the righteous and the wicked across ordinary areas of life, work, speech, wealth, and destiny, so that repeated exposure to the same pattern shapes a reader's instincts and self examination over time.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: These sayings are best read as general, typical descriptions of how life under God's moral order usually unfolds, not as unconditional legal promises. Verse 3, for instance, describes God's normal providential care for the righteous, but Job and Ecclesiastes both show cases where the righteous do go hungry or suffer in this life; the proverb states the ordinary rule, not an exception free guarantee, and Scripture's wisdom literature holds both truths together without contradiction.

debates.question: Does verse 3, 'the LORD does not let the righteous go hungry,' promise that no righteous person will ever suffer want?

views.label: A general proverbial pattern, not an absolute promise
views.case: Proverbs itself is proverbial wisdom, describing the normal shape of God's moral governance, while books like Job explicitly show a righteous man losing everything, and Psalm 37, closely related in theme, counsels patience precisely because the wicked can prosper and the righteous can suffer for a time.

views.label: A genuine assurance of God's providential care, read within its proper scope
views.case: The verse reflects a real and repeated biblical pattern of God's provision for His people, most vividly in the wilderness and in Christ's own teaching not to worry about food, Matthew 6:25-33, so it should be read as a trustworthy general truth about God's character, even where individual circumstances vary.

debates.bearing: The proverb is true and trustworthy as a description of God's normal, faithful care and moral government of the world, but like all proverbs it generalizes rather than guarantees every individual case; reading it alongside Job, Ecclesiastes, and the Psalms keeps its confidence in God's goodness from curdling into a false formula that blames every hungry righteous person for hidden sin.

study.originalLanguages: The chapter's engine is repetition, tsaddiq, the righteous, set against rasha, the wicked, verse after verse, letting contrast do the teaching that a sustained argument would do elsewhere. The same root, kacah, to cover or conceal, appears with opposite force in back to back verses, the wicked mouth conceals violence, verse 11, while love covers all transgressions, verse 12, a wordplay quoted directly in 1 Peter 4:8. And the chapter closes by returning to yirah, the fear of the LORD, verse 27, anchoring its many practical contrasts in the book's foundational reverence for God.

typology.fulfillment: Quoted directly in 1 Peter 4:8, and ultimately fulfilled in Christ's atoning love, which does not merely overlook sin but bears its penalty so it can be truly covered.

study.christ: This chapter's confident claims about the righteous, secure, blessed, a fountain of life, only find their full ground in Christ, since Scripture elsewhere insists no one is righteous on their own account, Romans 3:10. It is His righteousness, credited to those who trust Him, that qualifies anyone to stand truly in the blessed category this chapter describes. And its most quoted line, love covers all transgressions, verse 12, taken up directly in 1 Peter 4:8, points beyond ordinary neighborly forgiveness to the cross, where Christ's love did not simply overlook sin between people but paid its full cost so that it could be covered once for all.
study.god: God is the true source of lasting blessing, verse 22, and He undergirds the whole moral order this chapter assumes, providing for the righteous, denying the wicked's ruinous craving, and making His own fear, not cleverness or luck, the true key to a lengthened life, verse 27.

crossReferences.note: Above all, love covers a multitude of sins, quoting Proverbs 10:12 almost verbatim.
Out of the abundance of the heart the mouth speaks, echoing this chapter's many sayings connecting speech to inner character.
The same two way structure, the righteous flourishing and the wicked perishing, that organizes this entire chapter.
Through the obedience of the one man, many will be made righteous, grounding true righteousness in Christ rather than human achievement.

study.oneStory: This chapter's stream of contrasts between the righteous and the wicked distills the whole Bible's story of two ways into unforgettable, memorable lines. That story finds its resolution in Christ, whose perfect righteousness alone secures the blessings this chapter promises the righteous, and whose covering love, quoted directly from this chapter in 1 Peter 4:8, finally and fully deals with the sin no merely human covering could ever remove.

quiz.q: What literary form dominates chapter 10, and how does it differ from chapters 1 to 9?

options.text: Short, independent proverbs, mostly contrasting the righteous and the wicked, replacing the sustained discourses of chapters 1 to 9.
options.note: This marks the clear stylistic shift the heading in verse 1 signals.

options.text: Long narrative stories, just like chapters 1 to 9.
options.note: Chapter 10 moves away from narrative and sustained argument into short independent sayings.

options.text: Legal case rulings similar to the Law of Moses.
options.note: These are wisdom proverbs, not legal rulings or case law.

options.text: Personal letters addressed to named individuals.
options.note: The sayings are general proverbs, not personal correspondence, though many are addressed generally as if to a son elsewhere in the book.

quiz.q: How does verse 12 use the same Hebrew root, kacah, with opposite force compared to verse 11?

options.text: Verse 11 says the wicked mouth conceals violence, while verse 12 says love covers transgressions, the same word used to hide evil or graciously overlook wrong.
options.note: This wordplay is exact, and 1 Peter 4:8 quotes verse 12 directly.

options.text: The two verses use entirely unrelated words with no connection.
options.note: They share the same root, kacah, deliberately used with contrasting force.

options.text: Both verses describe the wicked exclusively.
options.note: Verse 12 specifically contrasts hatred with love, not two wicked descriptions.

options.text: Verse 12 is quoted in the Gospels, not in 1 Peter.
options.note: The direct New Testament quotation of this verse is in 1 Peter 4:8, not the Gospels.

quiz.q: How should proverbs like verse 3, 'the LORD does not let the righteous go hungry,' be understood?

options.text: As a general, typical description of God's moral order, not an unconditional promise against every exception.
options.note: Job and Ecclesiastes show real exceptions, so proverbs describe the normal pattern, not an ironclad guarantee for every case.

options.text: As an absolute guarantee with no exceptions anywhere in Scripture.
options.note: Job explicitly shows a righteous man suffering great want, contradicting a reading of total exception free guarantee.

options.text: As a statement with no real truth or usefulness at all.
options.note: The proverb reflects a real and trustworthy pattern of God's care, even though it is general rather than absolute.

options.text: As applicable only to New Testament believers, not Old Testament Israel.
options.note: The proverb's original audience was Israel, and its general principle of God's provision runs through both testaments, not one alone.

quiz.q: What does verse 27 identify as the key to a prolonged life?

options.text: The fear of the LORD.
options.note: This is stated directly and ties the chapter back to the book's opening motto in 1:7.

options.text: Financial wealth alone.
options.note: The verse specifically names the fear of the LORD, not wealth, as the key.

options.text: Physical strength and youth.
options.note: The verse's concern is moral and spiritual, not physical vigor.

options.text: Political power and influence.
options.note: The verse names fearing the LORD, not political standing, as decisive.

quiz.q: How does verse 12's teaching on love ultimately point to Christ?

options.text: Christ's atoning love does not merely overlook sin between people but bears its full penalty so it can be truly covered.
options.note: This connects the proverb, quoted in 1 Peter 4:8, to the deeper covering accomplished at the cross.

options.text: It has no connection to Christ or the New Testament.
options.note: 1 Peter 4:8 quotes this verse directly, showing a clear New Testament connection.

options.text: It teaches that love excuses sin without any real cost.
options.note: The gospel's covering of sin comes at real cost, the cross, not a mere excusing without payment.

options.text: It applies only to romantic relationships.
options.note: The proverb's scope is broader, covering wrongs between any people, and 1 Peter applies it to the whole Christian community.

questions: This chapter contrasts the righteous and the wicked in speech, work, and destiny. Which specific contrast in this chapter, honest versus deceptive speech, diligence versus laziness, hit closest to home for you today?

Verse 12 says love covers a multitude of sins, later quoted by Peter. Is there a wrong done against you that you are holding onto rather than covering with love, and what would it look like to release it?
Verse 19 warns that when words are many, sin is unavoidable. Where in your life could restraining your speech prevent unnecessary harm or conflict this week?
Verse 27 says the fear of the LORD prolongs life. How does reverence for God actually shape your daily choices, versus being just a phrase you agree with in theory?
This chapter's confidence in the righteous only holds fully true in Christ's righteousness credited to us. Where do you need to rest in His righteousness today rather than your own performance?

translationNotes.note: The second half of this verse can also be rendered 'but violence covers the mouth of the wicked,' a variation that reverses subject and verb but preserves the same basic contrast; the same variant reading applies to verse 11.
This verse can also be rendered 'but babbling fools will fall,' an alternate reading of the second half.
This verse is quoted nearly word for word in 1 Peter 4:8, 'above all, love covers a multitude of sins,' one of the clearest direct New Testament citations of Proverbs.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the makor-scripture toolkit for Proverbs 10:1-32. No em or en dashes present in this passage; none used anywhere in the file.
